preamble: The shift from the voiceless alveolar sibilant [s] to its voiced counterpart [z] is a standard form of voicing lenition. It typically occurs when the fricative is positioned between two voiced sounds (usually vowels), causing it to adopt the voicing of its environment to minimize articulatory effort.

examples.note: Intervocalic voicing of the sibilant is widespread in Romance languages.
Common sibilant voicing in French and Italian.
Voicing of the sibilant in specific phonetic contexts (pluralization).
Regressive voicing assimilation: /s/ becomes voiced [z] before a voiced consonant.

In Western Nuer, morpheme-final fricatives including /s/ undergo voicing lenition in intervocalic position as a morphologically conditioned process. The fricative phonemes alternate between voiceless and voiced allophones depending on morphological boundary position, with [z] appearing in morpheme-final (weak) position between voiced segments.

sources: Alkire, T., & Rosen, C. (2010). Romance Languages: A Historical Introduction. Cambridge University Press.
context: Intervocalic positions and before voiced consonants
allophones_note: /s/ surfaces as [z] in intervocalic position (Romance languages) or before voiced consonants (Hungarian), while [s] is preserved before voiceless consonants and in word-final position.
